text:
HEAD AND NECK MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

Patient: Margaret Chen | DOB: on file | MRN: on file
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Visit Type: Scheduled surveillance follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Chen is a 53-year-old Taiwanese-American woman and never-smoker presenting for her 12-month post-treatment surveillance visit following definitive concurrent cisplatin/IMRT for HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th ed.). She reports overall doing well with continued improvement in quality of life since our last visit.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen was diagnosed approximately 13 months ago with stage III HPV+ oropharyngeal SCC of the left base of tongue. She completed definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy — 70 Gy IMRT in 35 fractions with weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 (6 of 7 planned cycles delivered; one cycle held for transient nephrotoxicity, cumulative dose ~390 mg/m2) — which was completed without hospitalization during treatment, though she did require a brief 3-day inpatient admission approximately 2 weeks post-treatment for dehydration and pain management. She has been followed closely since treatment completion.

At her 12-week post-treatment restaging, she achieved a complete metabolic and radiographic response on both PET-CT and MRI neck. A prophylactic PEG tube placed prior to treatment was successfully removed approximately 5 months after treatment completion when oral intake had recovered to ~85% of needs. She has been progressively improving on all fronts.

She was also recently diagnosed with radiation-induced hypothyroidism approximately 10 months after completing treatment — her TSH had risen to 12.4 mIU/L with low free T4, consistent with grade 2 radiation-induced thyroid injury superimposed on her pre-existing mild hypothyroidism. Her levothyroxine was uptitrated from 50 mcg to 100 mcg daily, and she is now on that dose.

Interval since last visit: Approximately 6 weeks. She has had no new symptoms referable to the oropharynx — specifically no new throat pain, no odynophagia, no hemoptysis, no otalgia, and no new neck mass. She endorses mild persistent dry mouth (significantly improved from the treatment period) and some residual taste alteration. Fatigue has largely resolved. Swallowing is functional on a soft-to-regular diet. She has returned to full-time work as a high school principal and reports this with evident satisfaction.

No new complaints of dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis to suggest pulmonary involvement. No new bone pain. No neurologic symptoms.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Mild xerostomia (grade 1; improved). Mild dysgeusia — partial taste recovery, estimates ~60% of pre-treatment taste function restored. Occasional coughing with thin liquids in large boluses. Mild jaw stiffness (improving with exercises). Fatigue: essentially resolved. Hypothyroid symptoms fully resolved on adjusted levothyroxine dose.

Negative: No throat pain, no odynophagia, no new neck mass or swelling, no hemoptysis, no otalgia, no trismus at functional level, no voice change or hoarseness, no dyspnea, no weight loss, no night sweats, no fevers. No symptoms suggestive of carotid disease (no TIA symptoms, no syncope). No hearing deterioration noted subjectively.

---

MEDICATIONS (CURRENT)

- Levothyroxine 100 mcg PO daily (increased from 50 mcg at diagnosis; radiation-induced hypothyroidism + pre-existing hypothyroidism)
- Pilocarpine 5 mg PO TID (for xerostomia)
- Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GERD)
- Fluoride gel (prescription, topical; daily application in trays per dentistry recommendation)
- Oral magnesium glycinate 400 mg daily (ongoing supplementation post-cisplatin)
- Multivitamin daily
- Artificial saliva spray PRN

Allergies: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Appears at stated age. Comfortable.

Vital Signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 66 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.7°C.

Weight: 60.5 kg (essentially recovered to pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg; down <3%).

ECOG Performance Status: 0.

Head/Neck:
- Oral cavity: Mucosa moist; mild residual radiation-related mucosal pallor/atrophy on left pharyngeal wall; no ulceration, no leukoplakia, no erythema suspicious for recurrence. Salivary pooling: reduced but present. Dentition intact with no evidence of radiation caries.
- Oropharynx: On direct inspection, base of tongue without visible mass or ulceration; post-radiation mucosal thickening present, stable in appearance compared to prior exams; no new lesion visible.
- Jaw: Mouth opening 40 mm (pre-treatment 42 mm; grade 1 trismus, improving). Temporomandibular joint non-tender.
- Neck: No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No palpable masses. The previously enlarged left level IIA node is not palpable. Mild firmness/fibrosis in the left anterior neck consistent with radiation-related soft tissue changes. A soft bruit is audible over the left carotid on auscultation; right carotid without audible bruit. Thyroid: not enlarged on palpation.
- Skin: Neck skin shows mild residual hyperpigmentation bilaterally in the radiation field; no moist desquamation, no ulceration, no fibrosis of skin folds clinically significant.

Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic today by attending):
- Nasal cavities: clear bilaterally.
- Nasopharynx: Normal mucosa; no mass.
- Oropharynx and base of tongue: Post-radiation mucosal changes present — mild edema and superficial mucosal irregularity of left base of tongue consistent with known treatment effect; NO discrete mass, NO mucosal ulceration, NO abnormal vascular pattern, NO submucosal fullness to suggest recurrence. Mucosa stable compared to prior scoping at 6-month visit.
- Supraglottis/larynx: Normal epiglottis; arytenoid mucosa intact; bilateral true vocal cord mobility full and symmetric; no subglottic mass.
- Piriform sinuses: Clear bilaterally.
- Overall: No endoscopic evidence of recurrence. Post-treatment changes stable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No S3/S4.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly.

Extremities: No edema.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC (drawn today):
- WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 12.9 g/dL (mildly low, stable; likely residual effect of prior CRT and/or mild subclinical hypothyroidism), Plt 218 x10^9/L.

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- Cr 0.72 mg/dL (baseline; excellent renal recovery post-cisplatin). BUN 14. Na 139, K 4.0, CO2 25.
- LFTs: AST 22, ALT 18, Alk phos 88, total bilirubin 0.5 — all within normal limits.
- Albumin 4.1 g/dL.

Thyroid function:
- TSH 2.1 mIU/L (within target range on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day; well-controlled). Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal).

Magnesium: 2.0 mEq/L (now within normal range on oral supplementation; improved from cisplatin-related depletion).

Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

MRI neck with contrast (annual surveillance, performed approximately 1 week prior to today's visit):
No evidence of locoregional recurrence. The left base of tongue demonstrates stable post-treatment mucosal thickening and mild enhancement, unchanged from prior MRI at 12 weeks. No new mass, no abnormal nodal enlargement. The previously noted small left level IIA residual soft tissue density (8 mm on 12-week scan) has further decreased and now measures 5 mm — not FDG-avid on concurrent PET component; consistent with ongoing fibrotic involution, not recurrence. Radiation-related soft tissue changes in the neck are stable. Mild bilateral carotid atherosclerotic changes without significant stenosis noted. No radiation necrosis of the mandible or pharyngeal soft tissues. Impression: No evidence of locoregional recurrence; stable post-treatment changes at 12 months. (Full report on file.)

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE (NED) AT 12 MONTHS

Ms. Chen continues to demonstrate a complete and durable response to definitive concurrent cisplatin/IMRT. Annual MRI neck is negative for recurrence. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy today shows no mucosal evidence of recurrence. Clinical exam without palpable adenopathy. She is now 12 months from treatment completion and remains NED — an excellent milestone, particularly given the already-favorable prognosis conferred by her HPV-positive, never-smoker status with T2N1M0 disease.

- Surveillance: Continue per established protocol. Next visit in approximately 3-4 months (approximately 15-16 months post-treatment). Annual MRI neck to continue.
- Chest surveillance imaging: Per institutional protocol, will order chest CT without contrast at the 18-month mark for distant metastasis and secondary primary surveillance (she is a never-smoker, so lung cancer risk is low, but secondary primaries in HNSCC patients warrant monitoring).
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: To be performed at each clinic visit.

2. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM

She was diagnosed with grade 2 radiation-induced hypothyroidism approximately 10 months post-treatment. Currently on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. TSH today 2.1 mIU/L — excellent. She is asymptomatic from a thyroid standpoint. Her pre-existing hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine 50 mcg at baseline) has clearly been compounded by radiation injury to the thyroid. She will require lifelong thyroid monitoring.
- Continue levothyroxine 100 mcg daily.
- Recheck TSH in 4-6 months at next surveillance visit.
- Endocrinology is involved for co-management given prior hypothyroid history plus radiation injury.

3. XEROSTOMIA — GRADE 1, WELL-MANAGED

Significant improvement from grade 2-3 during and immediately post-treatment. She is managing well with pilocarpine 5 mg TID and liberal water intake. Artificial saliva spray used PRN.
- Continue pilocarpine at current dose.
- Encourage continued oral hydration.
- Reassess pilocarpine need at 18-month visit; may trial dose reduction if xerostomia continues to improve.
- Dental follow-up ongoing; fluoride trays in use — strongly reinforce, as xerostomia-related dental caries can be a long-term morbidity.

4. DYSGEUSIA — GRADE 1, IMPROVING

Approximately 60% taste recovery at 12 months. This is expected to continue to improve gradually over 18-24 months post-treatment. Zinc supplementation can be offered, though evidence is modest.
- Discussed zinc supplementation 25 mg daily as option; patient will consider.
- Reassure that further taste recovery is expected.

5. TRISMUS — GRADE 1, IMPROVING

Mouth opening 40 mm (pre-treatment 42 mm). Jaw exercises ongoing. She is close to pre-treatment baseline.
- Continue daily jaw-stretching exercises and Therabite device use.
- If mouth opening begins to decrease, physical/occupational therapy referral for progressive mechanical stretching.

6. DYSPHAGIA — FUNCTIONAL

Tolerating a regular soft diet with thin liquids. Occasional coughing with large-bolus thin liquids — consistent with the mild pharyngeal dysmotility and trace aspiration on thin liquids seen on modified barium swallow prior to PEG removal. She elected not to use thickened liquids given subjective tolerability.
- Continue swallowing exercises (per speech therapy program).
- Advise continued caution with large liquid boluses — smaller sips.
- Repeat modified barium swallow at 18-month visit to reassess swallowing function objectively.

7. CAROTID SURVEILLANCE

Soft bruit detected on left carotid today (also noted at 6-month visit). Carotid duplex ultrasound was completed at approximately 14 months from diagnosis per vascular surgery recommendation — showed mild bilateral carotid intimal-medial thickening without hemodynamically significant stenosis. Radiation-related accelerated atherosclerosis is a known long-term risk in patients receiving neck irradiation.
- Continue annual carotid duplex surveillance per vascular surgery.
- Counsel regarding cardiovascular risk factor optimization (blood pressure well-controlled, BMI near normal, never-smoker — favorable profile).
- Aspirin 81 mg daily discussed; deferred to primary care and vascular surgery for decision given no compelling indication at present.

8. OTOTOXICITY — GRADE 1, STABLE

Repeat audiogram performed today at this visit (audiology performed prior to clinic appointment):
- Results: Bilateral high-frequency threshold shift at 4 kHz, 10-15 dB, unchanged from 18-month audiogram at end of treatment. No progression. Speech discrimination scores intact. No subjective hearing impairment reported.
- No intervention required. Annual audiologic monitoring to continue.
- Counsel to avoid prolonged noise exposure and ototoxic medications (e.g., loop diuretics, aminoglycosides) given underlying subclinical cisplatin-related hearing injury.

9. QUALITY OF LIFE AND SURVIVORSHIP

Ms. Chen continues to demonstrate excellent psychological recovery and has returned to full-time work as a high school principal. She has participated in a head and neck cancer survivorship support group. The predominant ongoing quality-of-life concerns are minor xerostomia and residual taste alteration, both of which are improving. She reports feeling largely back to normal.
- Referral to survivorship clinic maintained.
- Discussed ongoing HPV awareness; counseled regarding HPV vaccination for adolescent children and family members.
- Discussed importance of sun protection and oral hygiene in the context of long-term radiation sequelae.
- ECOG PS 0 at this visit.

Time spent: 45 minutes (greater than 50% of encounter time in counseling and coordination of care).

Return to clinic: Approximately 3-4 months (15-16 months post-treatment completion).

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist, Head and Neck Medical Oncology]
